text: The laparoscopic ovarian cystectomy procedure begins, with the surgical team preparing to access the ovarian cyst using minimally invasive techniques.

The surgeon performs hydrodissection of the left ovary using a dilution of vasopressin with normal saline, separating the ovarian cyst from the ovarian cortex. A hemorrhagic cyst is visible on the left ovary.
The surgeon uses monopolar energy to incise the ovarian cortex following hydrodissection with a vasopressin and normal saline solution, targeting the left ovarian hemorrhagic cyst.

The surgeon uses graspers to separate the ovarian cortex from the left ovarian hemorrhagic cyst. The encapsulated old blood within the cyst is visible as the cyst is carefully detached from the ovary without complications.
The dilution of vasopressin also help us for hemostasis. In case that we need to do hemostasis we can use the bipolar instrument.
The surgeon performs hydrodissection of the right ovary using a laparoscopic needle to inject a dilution of vasopressin mixed with normal saline between the ovarian cortex and the ovarian cyst.
because it will facilitate the dissection of the cyst without any complications and also will help us for hemostasis since we're using vasopressing.

The surgeon performs an incision on the ovarian cortex using monopolar energy, facilitating the separation of the ovarian cortex from the ovarian cyst. The ovarian cortex is grasped, and the right ovarian cyst is shielded out, aided by the vasopressin dilution.
The surgeon is handling a right ovarian dermoid cyst, visible within the surgical field.
The surgeon identifies multiple cysts within the ovarian cortex, focusing on the dermoid cyst, which is being dissected and removed from the ovary.
The surgeon continues to shell out the right ovarian dermoid cyst from the ovary, with visible assistance from the vasopressin dilution aiding in hemostasis and facilitating the dissection process.
